The ACA Medicaid Expansion and Health Insurance Coverage: A Staggered Difference-in-Differences Analysis

Author

Colin Heeneman

Published

March 10, 2026

Introduction

The Affordable Care Act (ACA) of 2010 included a provision to expand Medicaid eligibility to adults with incomes up to 138% of the federal poverty level. The 2012 Supreme Court ruling in NFIB v. Sebelius made expansion optional for states, creating a natural experiment: states chose to expand at different times — or not at all — for reasons largely unrelated to the underlying health of their populations. This staggered adoption allows credible causal identification of expansion’s effect on health coverage and access to care.

This analysis uses individual-level data from the American Community Survey (ACS) and state-level data from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) to estimate two outcomes: (1) the uninsured rate among non-elderly adults, and (2) the share of adults reporting they could not see a doctor due to cost in the past year. The primary estimator is the Callaway and Sant’Anna (2021) staggered difference-in-differences (DiD) method, which addresses the well-documented bias of two-way fixed effects (TWFE) under heterogeneous treatment timing (Goodman-Bacon 2021; Sun and Abraham 2021).

Using a Callaway-Sant’Anna staggered DiD estimator with never-expanding states as the comparison group, the analysis finds that Medicaid expansion reduced the uninsured rate among all non-elderly adults by 2.3 percentage points (95% CI: −3.5 to −1.1 pp) and by 5.1 percentage points among adults below 200% of the federal poverty level (95% CI: −7.0 to −3.2 pp). These estimates are statistically significant and stable across multiple robustness checks. Cost-related care avoidance declined by 0.6 percentage points post-expansion, consistent with the predicted downstream effect of coverage gains, though the estimate is not statistically significant given limited BRFSS pre-treatment years.

Background

Prior to the ACA, Medicaid primarily covered low-income children, pregnant women, and disabled individuals. Non-elderly adults without dependent children were largely ineligible regardless of income. The ACA’s Medicaid expansion extended coverage to all adults below 138% FPL, with the federal government financing 100% of expansion costs through 2016 and 90% thereafter.

Early adopters (primarily 2014) saw immediate, large increases in coverage. A growing body of research documents reductions in the uninsured rate (Frean, Gruber, and Sommers 2017), improvements in financial protection (Finkelstein et al. 2012), and reductions in cost-related care avoidance (Sommers, Baicker, and Epstein 2015). The present analysis contributes to this literature by applying the Callaway and Sant’Anna (2021) estimator, which uses only variation within clean comparison groups and avoids contaminated comparisons that arise in canonical TWFE when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts.

Data

American Community Survey

Individual-level data come from the ACS 1-year public-use microdata samples for 2010–2019, obtained via IPUMS USA (Ruggles et al. 2024). The sample is restricted to non-elderly adults aged 18–64 with valid person weights. The primary outcome is a binary indicator for having no health insurance coverage (HCOVANY == 1). A secondary specification restricts to adults with household incomes below 200% of the federal poverty level (POVERTY < 200), the population most directly affected by the expansion’s eligibility threshold. State-year uninsured rates are computed as survey-weighted proportions.

Code 
panel |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(G == 0, "Never Expanded", "Expanded")) |>
  group_by(year, group) |>
  summarise(mean_uninsured = mean(uninsured_rate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mean_uninsured, color = group, linetype = group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2013.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "grey50") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Expanded" = "#3182bd", "Never Expanded" = "#e6550d")) +
  labs(
    title  = "Uninsured Rate by Medicaid Expansion Status",
    x      = NULL, y = "Mean uninsured rate", color = NULL, linetype = NULL,
    caption = "Note: Dotted line marks 2014, primary expansion year. Source: ACS."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

BRFSS Cost Avoidance

State-level estimates of cost-related care avoidance come from the CDC’s Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, which annually asks respondents whether there was a time in the past 12 months when they needed to see a doctor but could not because of cost. Data cover 2011–2019. The BRFSS transitioned to raking weights in 2011, introducing a methodological discontinuity that makes 2010 estimates not directly comparable to subsequent years; the BRFSS panel therefore begins in 2011, providing three pre-expansion years for the cost avoidance outcome.

Treatment Data

Medicaid expansion dates are drawn from Kaiser Family Foundation State Health Facts, cross-referenced against Frean, Gruber, and Sommers (2017). States expanding after 2019 — Idaho, Nebraska, Utah, Oklahoma, and Missouri — are coded as never-treated within the analysis window because no post-treatment observations are available. This leaves 17 never-expanding states as the primary comparison group.

Code 
panel |>
  filter(!duplicated(statefip)) |>
  filter(G > 0) |>
  count(G) |>
  arrange(G) |>
  mutate(cumulative = cumsum(n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = G, y = cumulative)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 1, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#3182bd") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2014:2019) +
  labs(
    title = "Cumulative Medicaid Expansion Adoption",
    x = "Year", y = "Number of states expanded"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

Empirical Strategy

Identification

The core identifying assumption is parallel trends: in the absence of expansion, uninsured rates in expansion states would have followed the same trajectory as in non-expansion states. The pre-treatment event study estimates serve as the primary diagnostic. Conditional on parallel pre-trends, expansion is as good as randomly assigned within the Callaway and Sant’Anna (2021) framework.

Callaway-Sant’Anna Estimator

The canonical TWFE regression — regressing outcomes on a post-expansion dummy with state and year fixed effects — is known to produce biased estimates under staggered adoption when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or over time (Goodman-Bacon 2021). The Callaway and Sant’Anna (2021) estimator addresses this by computing group-time average treatment effects \(ATT(g,t)\) — the average effect for cohort \(g\) at time \(t\) — using only clean comparisons against never-treated units. These are then aggregated into a single summary ATT and a dynamic event-study path.

Formally, let \(G_i\) denote the year state \(i\) first expanded Medicaid (with \(G_i = \infty\) for never-expanders). The group-time ATT is:

\[ATT(g,t) = E[Y_{it}(g) - Y_{it}(\infty) \mid G_i = g]\]

Aggregated across all cohorts and post-treatment periods using group size weights, this yields the simple aggregate ATT reported in the main results. Standard errors are clustered at the state level throughout.

TWFE Baseline

For comparability with the prior literature, TWFE estimates are also reported:

\[Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \delta \cdot \text{Treated}_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}\]

where \(\alpha_i\) are state fixed effects, \(\lambda_t\) are year fixed effects, and \(\text{Treated}_{it} = \mathbf{1}[G_i \leq t]\) is a binary post-expansion indicator. Standard errors are clustered at the state level.

Results

Primary Outcome: Uninsured Rate

Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here("output", "figures", "es_uninsured_all.png"))
Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here("output", "figures", "es_uninsured_low.png"))

Secondary Outcome: Cost-Related Care Avoidance

Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here("output", "figures", "es_cost_avoidance.png"))

Summary ATT Estimates

Code 
att_results <- tibble(
  Outcome = c(
    "Uninsured rate (all adults)",
    "Uninsured rate (low-income adults)",
    "Cost avoidance (BRFSS)"
  ),
  ATT  = c(
    model_obj$agg_uninsured$overall.att,
    model_obj$agg_uninsured_low$overall.att,
    model_obj$agg_cost$overall.att
  ),
  SE   = c(
    model_obj$agg_uninsured$overall.se,
    model_obj$agg_uninsured_low$overall.se,
    model_obj$agg_cost$overall.se
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    CI_lo = ATT - 1.96 * SE,
    CI_hi = ATT + 1.96 * SE,
    across(c(ATT, SE, CI_lo, CI_hi), ~ round(.x, 4))
  )

kable(att_results, col.names = c("Outcome", "ATT", "SE", "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper"),
      booktabs = TRUE) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Aggregate ATT estimates, Callaway-Sant’Anna (2021)
Outcome ATT SE 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper
Uninsured rate (all adults) -0.0229 0.0060 -0.0346 -0.0113
Uninsured rate (low-income adults) -0.0512 0.0097 -0.0702 -0.0323
Cost avoidance (BRFSS) -0.0056 0.0042 -0.0139 0.0027

TWFE vs. CS Comparison

Code 
modelsummary(
  list("TWFE" = model_obj$twfe_uninsured),
  stars = TRUE,
  gof_omit = "AIC|BIC|Log|F|RMSE",
  coef_rename = c("post_treated" = "Medicaid Expansion")
)

Robustness

Code 
rob <- tibble(
  Specification = c(
    "Baseline (reg estimator)",
    "Exclude mid-year adopters",
    "Doubly-robust estimator",
    "Exclude CA, TX, FL, NY"
  ),
  ATT = c(
    model_obj$agg_uninsured$overall.att,
    model_obj$agg_nomid$overall.att,
    model_obj$agg_dr$overall.att,
    model_obj$agg_nolarge$overall.att
  ),
  SE = c(
    model_obj$agg_uninsured$overall.se,
    model_obj$agg_nomid$overall.se,
    model_obj$agg_dr$overall.se,
    model_obj$agg_nolarge$overall.se
  )
) |>
  mutate(across(c(ATT, SE), ~ round(.x, 4)))

kable(rob, col.names = c("Specification", "ATT", "SE"), booktabs = TRUE) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Robustness checks: Aggregate ATT, uninsured rate
Specification ATT SE
Baseline (reg estimator) -0.0229 0.0060
Exclude mid-year adopters -0.0228 0.0061
Doubly-robust estimator -0.0229 0.0061
Exclude CA, TX, FL, NY -0.0242 0.0067

Discussion

The results indicate that Medicaid expansion produced meaningful reductions in the uninsured rate, with effects concentrated among the low-income population the policy was designed to reach. The aggregate ATT of −5.1 percentage points for adults below 200% FPL is somewhat smaller than the estimates reported by Frean, Gruber, and Sommers (2017), who find reductions of approximately 10 percentage points using a different identification strategy that pools expansion and near-expansion states as controls. The more conservative estimate here reflects the use of strictly never-expanding states as the comparison group — a cleaner but more demanding identification assumption that excludes the late-adopter states used in some prior work.

The event study plots provide strong support for the parallel trends assumption. For the primary uninsured rate outcomes, pre-expansion estimates converge cleanly to zero by the year prior to expansion, with the post-expansion path showing an immediate, sustained decline. This pattern — flat pre-trends followed by a sharp level shift at \(t = 0\) — is the canonical signature of a credible difference-in-differences design. The low-income subgroup plot is particularly clean, with pre-expansion estimates essentially at zero for three consecutive years before the sharp post-expansion drop.

The cost avoidance results are directionally consistent with the coverage findings but are not statistically significant. Several factors likely contribute to the wider confidence intervals for this outcome. First, the BRFSS panel begins in 2011, providing only three pre-expansion years compared to four in the ACS-based outcomes. Second, the BRFSS question captures self-reported behavior rather than administrative coverage status, introducing additional measurement noise. Third, the conversion of insurance coverage gains into reduced care avoidance may operate with some lag, as newly-insured individuals establish care relationships. The post-expansion event study path shows a gradual, sustained drift toward lower cost avoidance from \(t = +1\) through \(t = +5\), consistent with this mechanism even in the absence of statistical significance.

Several limitations warrant note. First, the parallel trends assumption requires that expansion and non-expansion states would have followed the same coverage trajectory absent the policy change. Never-expanding states are disproportionately Southern and politically conservative, and may have faced different structural trends in insurance markets. While the flat pre-expansion estimates are reassuring, this assumption cannot be directly tested. Second, the did package flags small cohort sizes for the 2015, 2016, and 2019 adoption cohorts (two to three states each), limiting the precision of cohort-specific estimates; the 2014 cohort of 27 states drives the aggregate result. Third, the analysis covers non-elderly adults aged 18–64; effects on children and the elderly, who face different Medicaid eligibility rules, are not estimated here.

Conclusion

This analysis provides evidence that state Medicaid expansion under the ACA causally reduced health insurance uninsurance among non-elderly adults, with a statistically significant aggregate effect of 2.3 percentage points for the full adult population and 5.1 percentage points for adults below 200% of the federal poverty level. These findings are robust to the exclusion of mid-year adopters, the use of a doubly-robust estimator, and the removal of the four largest states. Cost-related care avoidance declined in the post-expansion period in the expected direction, consistent with coverage gains translating into improved access, though this estimate does not reach conventional significance thresholds.

The methodological contribution of this analysis lies in its application of the Callaway and Sant’Anna (2021) staggered DiD estimator, which avoids the negative-weighting bias that afflicts canonical TWFE under heterogeneous treatment timing (Goodman-Bacon 2021). The near-identical estimates produced by the outcome regression and doubly-robust specifications, combined with the stable robustness checks, suggest the results are not sensitive to functional form assumptions. Together, the evidence supports the conclusion that Medicaid expansion achieved its primary goal of reducing the uninsured rate among low-income adults, with effects that persisted for at least five years post-adoption.

References

Callaway, Brantly, and Pedro H. C. Sant’Anna. 2021. “Difference-in-Differences with Multiple Time Periods.” Journal of Econometrics 225 (2): 200–230. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2020.12.001.
Finkelstein, Amy, Sarah Taubman, Bill Wright, Mira Bernstein, Jonathan Gruber, Joseph P. Newhouse, Heidi Allen, and Katherine Baicker. 2012. “The Oregon Health Insurance Experiment: Evidence from the First Year.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 127 (3): 1057–1106. https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjs020.
Frean, Molly, Jonathan Gruber, and Benjamin D. Sommers. 2017. “Premium Subsidies, the Mandate, and Medicaid Expansion: Coverage Effects of the Affordable Care Act.” Journal of Health Economics 53: 72–86. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhealeco.2017.02.004.
Goodman-Bacon, Andrew. 2021. “Difference-in-Differences with Variation in Treatment Timing.” Journal of Econometrics 225 (2): 254–77. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2021.03.014.
Ruggles, Steven, Sarah Flood, Matthew Sobek, Daniel Backman, Annie Chen, Grace Cooper, Stephanie Richards, Renae Rogers, and Megan Schouweiler. 2024. “IPUMS USA: Version 14.0.” Minneapolis, MN: IPUMS. https://doi.org/10.18128/D010.V14.0.
Sommers, Benjamin D., Katherine Baicker, and Arnold M. Epstein. 2015. “Changes in Utilization and Health Among Low-Income Adults After Medicaid Expansion or Expanded Private Insurance.” JAMA Internal Medicine 175 (3): 418–26. https://doi.org/10.1001/jamainternmed.2014.8040.
Sun, Liyang, and Sarah Abraham. 2021. “Estimating Dynamic Treatment Effects in Event Studies with Heterogeneous Treatment Effects.” Journal of Econometrics 225 (2): 175–99. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2020.09.006.
